title: Pascoe & Anor v Minister for Land Information
text:
PASCOE & ANOR v MINISTER FOR LAND INFORMATION [2025] NZSC 195 [18 December 2025]   
IN THE  SUPREME COURT  OF NEW ZEALAND  
 
I TE KŌTI MANA NUI O AOTEAROA  
 SC 123/2024  
 [2025]  NZSC  195  
  
BETWEEN   
TONY JAMES SOFUS PASCOE AND 
DEBBIE  ANN PASCOE  
Appellant s 
  
AND   
MINISTER FOR LAND INFORMATION  
Respondent  
 
Hearing:   
14 October 2025  
 
Court:   
Winkelmann CJ, Glazebrook, Ellen France, Kós and Miller JJ  
 
Counsel:   
Appellants in person  
R L Roff and E S Harris for Respondent  
S R Gepp KC and M C Wright as counsel assisting the Court  
 
Judgment:   
18 December 2025  
 
 
JUDGMENT OF THE COURT  
 
A The application of counsel assisting the Court for leave to 
adduce further evidence is granted.  
B The Court answers the question for which leave was 
granted in the following way: the negotiations function under s  18(1)(d) of the Public Works Act 1981 may be 
performed by an accredited contractor, rather than the Minister personally or the Minister’s delegate within Toitū  Te Whenua | Land Information New Zealand  (LINZ) , 
provided the accredited contractor holds delegated authority to perform that function.  
C The Court finds that LINZ officials delegated the negotiations function outside the Public Service in this case but did not follow the necessary statutory processes.  
The appellants  did not refuse to negotiate, for purposes of 
s 18(2), by refusing to deal with the accredited contractor . 
D The appeal is allowed accordingly.  The proceeding is remitted to the High Court to determine what relief, if any, is appropriate.

E The respondent must pay the appellants one set of costs of 
$3,000 plus usual disbursements.  
____________________________________________________________________ 
 
REASONS

Introduction  
[1] Private land that is required for a government work may be compulsorily 
acquired by the Minister for Land Information under the Public Works Act 1981.

But the Minister must first attempt to negotiate its purchase with the owner.  Only if 
the owner refuses to negotiate, or negotiations fail, may the land be taken.  
The question in this case is whether the landowners, Tony and Debbie Pascoe, refused 
to negotiate with officials exercising the Minister’s delegated authority, so empowering the Minister to take their land.  
[2] The Act prescribes that the Minister must give notice of the Government’s 
desire to acquire the land and “invite the owner to sell the land to him”.
1  The Minister 
must then “make every endeavour to negotiate in good faith with the owner in an 
attempt to reach an agreement for the acquisition of the land”.2  If after three months 
the owner “fails to respond” to the Minister’s invitation to sell the land or “refuses to 
negotiate with the Minister”, or an agreement is not reached, the Minister may 
“proceed to take the land” under the Act’s processes.3  The Minister triggers the 
compulsory acquisition by issuing a notice of intention to take under s  23(1) of the 
Act. 
[3] The Pascoes own a farm in the Mangapepeke Valley in northern Taranaki.  
State Highway 3 crosses Mt Messenger to the south of the farm.  The  New Zealand 
Transport Agency | Waka Kotahi (NZTA) is building a bypass which will take the 
highway around Mt Messenger and through the Pascoes’ land.4  We will call this “the 
project”.  The Minister wants to acquire 11.1715 ha of their land and to lease a further 
area of 12.7489 ha during the construction phase.  
[4] Toitū Te Whenua | Land Information New Zealand (LINZ) is a government 
department to whose officials successive Ministers have delegated their powers or 
functions to give notice of the Government’s desire to acquire land, and to negotiate 
its purchase, and to execute agreements for sale and purchase that acquire land by 
agreement under the Act’s provisions.5 
 
1  Public Works Act 1981, s 18(1)(a) and (c).  
2  Section 18(1)(d).  
3  Section 18(2).  
4  NZTA is a Crown entity under the Crown Entities Act 2004: see s  7(1)(a) and sch 1 pt 1; and 
Land  Transport Management Act 2003, s  93. 
5  LINZ is a public service department under the Public Service Act 2020: see sch 2 pt 1.  
The Minister for Land Information is the Hon Chris Penk but the decisions made in this case were 
made by previous ministers.

[5] NZTA, as the Crown acquiring agency responsible for the project, appointed 
The Property Group Ltd (TPG) to negotiate with a number of landowners for the 
acquisition of the necessary land.  TPG is a privately owned company and an 
accredited supplier, meanin g generally that it has been accredited by LINZ to 
undertake certain work in connection with the acquisition and disposal of  land for 
public works. 
[6] LINZ gave the Pascoes notice of the Minister’s desire to acquire the land.  
TPG  then approached the Pascoes to seek their agreement, but they declined to engage 
with TPG.  They insisted on negotiating with the Minister for Land Information or 
with LINZ off icials.  
[7] This appeal comes before us on a question of law stated by the High Court in 
the Pascoes’ application for judicial review of the Minister’s notice of intention to take 
the land following the failure of negotiations.6  The question as framed in this Court’s 
decision granting leave to appeal, from the decision of the Court of Appeal on that preliminary question, was whether negotiations for the compulsory acquisition of land 
for essential works under s 18 “may be undertaken by an accredited contractor” rather 
than the Minister personally or the Minister’s delegate within LINZ.
7  We return to the 
approved question below at [71] . 
[8] The application for judicial review has proceeded in the meantime, 
unsuccessfully for the Pascoes,8 and it is now before the Court of Appeal.  
 
6  Pascoe v Minister for Land Information HC New Plymouth CIV -2021 -443-48, 29 April 2022 
(Minute of Ellis J) at [8].  The object was to deal with the application for judicial review, which 
contained six causes of action (at that stage) potentially requiring a good deal of evidence, in the 
most expeditious manner.  
7  Pascoe v Minister for Land Information [2025] NZSC 54 (Glazebrook, Kós and Miller JJ) 
[SC leave judgment].  LINZ describes accredited contractors as “accredited suppliers”, but we 
refer to them interchangeably.  
8  Pascoe v Minister for Land Information [2025] NZHC 1783 (McQueen J).  The Judge held that 
the decision of the Court of Appeal on the questions of law (which is the subject of the present appeal) had determined three causes of action, subject to the “limited appeal” to this Court: at  [41].  
McQu een J decided the remaining causes of action, relating to alleged failures to assess the scope 
and effects of the project and the area actually required for it, to provide pre -approval to fund a 
report to assess damage to date to the Pascoes and their land , and to supply the Minister with 
information and advice necessary to inform the Minister’s decision to sign the s 23 notice.  Isac  J 
had earlier determined a cause of action as a preliminary question of law concerning the delegation of the s  18(1)(a) powe r: Pascoe v Minister for Land Information (No 2)  [2023] NZHC 2844.  
That decision was not appealed.

An application for leave to appeal direct to this Court was abandoned.  We discuss the 
implications of our decision for the High Court proceeding below from [99].9 
[9] The Environment Court has also heard the Pascoes’ substantive objection to 
the s 23(1) notice of intention to take the land.10  The Court found that the taking was 
fair, sound and reasonably necessary.  The High Court dismissed an appeal, which 
could be brought only on a question of law.11  That decision is not before us either.  
We declined leave for a direct appeal from that High  Court judgment.12  It is now the 
subject of a pending application for leave to appeal to the Court of Appeal on a 
question of law.13 
[10] The significance of this appeal is that the Pascoes maintain the Minister never 
negotiated with them and so never acquired the right to proceed to take the land in the 
first place.  That being so, they say, everything that has happened in the interim must 
be set aside and the Minister must come to the negotiating table.  They maintain that 
they were and remain willing to negotiate with the Minister or his LINZ delegates but 
the offers made have been incomplete and inadequate.  
[11] The Minister’s response is that his function of negotiating with the Pascoes 
was not delegated to TPG but was exercised by LINZ officials, who relied on TPG to 
do much of the “legwork” or day- to-day conduct of negotiations.  He maintains that 
the Pascoes could not insist on dealing directly with LINZ, and his delegates told them 
that they must deal with TPG.  That being so, the Minister acquired the right to issue 
a notice of intention to take the land when they refused to deal with TPG.  The Minister 
adds that the notice of intention does not preclude renewed negotiations.  
 
9  See also below at [78] . 
10  Pascoe v Minister for Land Information [2024] NZEnvC 101 .  See Public Works Act, ss 23(3) 
and 24. 
11  Pascoe v Minister for Land Information [2025] NZHC 1782.  See s  24(14); and Resource 
Management Act 1991 , s 299.  
12  Pascoe v Minister for Land Information [2025] NZSC 114.  
13  Resource Management Act , s 308; and Criminal Procedure Act 2011, s  303.  The Pascoes have 
also sought an extension of time to apply for leave to appeal to the Court of Appeal.

Outline  
[12] We begin with the legislation, to identify the Minister’s duties, functions and 
powers.  We then turn to the concept of negotiations, not to suggest that they must 
follow any particular process but to ascertain what the legislation tells us about what 
they must or may encompass.  That sets part of the context for an assessment of the 
Pascoes’ claim that the Minister’s conduct amounted in fact to a delegation of his statutory negotiations function to someone who never enjoyed delegated authority.  
[13] We next examine the topic of delegation.  It is common ground that the 
Minister has lawfully delegated the negotiations function to LINZ officials. 
The Minister does not defend his actions by asserting that the function was lawfully 
further delegated to TP G.  It is nonetheless necessary to explain what delegations are 
authorised, and to whom.  Only then is it possible to see the dealings among LINZ, 
NZTA, TPG and the Pascoes in context. 
[14] We next outline the facts found in the record before us. 
[15] We then return to the appeal, to isolate the issues for decision and briefly 
summarise the judgments of the Courts below.  We finally address those issues, quite 
shortly.  As will be seen, there is also a question of remedy, should we agree with the 
Pascoes that they did not “refuse to negotiate with the Minister” for purposes of s 18. 
The legislation  
[16] We outline the principal features of the statutory scheme for acquisition of land 
for a government work, meaning relevantly a work to be constructed by the Crown for 
any public purpose.
14  The Act empowers the Minister to acquire any land required for 
any government work.15 
 
14  The term “Government work” is defined in s 2 of the Public Works Act.  The right to take land 
under the Act also extends to local authorities for works that they are to construct, but we confine 
ourselves to government works.  It is not in dispute that the project is a government work.  
15  Section 4A(a).  It is now the Minister for Land Information who is the Minister responsible for 
the relevant parts of the Act and who exercises the ministerial powers under the Act: see s 4C(2); Dromgool v Minister for Land Information [2022] NZSC 157, [2022] 1 NZLR 716 at [17], n 16 
per William Young, Glazebrook, O’Regan and Ellen France JJ and [122] per Winkelmann  CJ; and 
Legislation Act 2019, s 11 and s 13 definition of “Minister”.

[17] Under s 17(1) the Minister may “enter into an agreement to purchase any land 
for any public work”.16  The Minister need not invoke compulsory acquisition powers 
before acquiring land by agreement under s 17.17 
[18] Section 18(1) sets out four steps that the Minister must take before proceeding 
to take land compulsorily under the Act’s processes:  
18 Prior negotiations required for acquisition of land for essential 
works  
(1) Where any land is required for any  public  work the Minister or local 
authority, as the case may be, shall, before proceeding to take the land 
under this Act — 
 (a) serve notice of his or its desire to acquire the land on every 
person having a registered interest in the land; and  
 (b) lodge a notice of desire to acquire the land with 
the Registrar -General of Land who shall register it, without 
fee, against the  record of title  affected; and  
 (c) invite the owner to sell the land to him or it, and, following a 
valuation carried out by a registered valuer, advise the owner 
of the estimated amount of compensation to which he would 
be entitled under this Act or the betterment that he may be 
liable to pay; and  
 (d) make every endeavour to negotiate in good faith with the 
owner in an attempt to reach an agreement for the acquisition 
of the land.  
It will be seen that the Minister must notify the owner of the desire to acquire the land, 
invite the owner to sell, and make every endeavour to negotiate.  The object is that an 
agreement for sale and purchase will be entered under s 17.  
[19] Section 18(2) provides for what may happen if, after three months, the owner 
fails to respond to the Minister’s invitation to sell, or the owner refuses to negotiate 
with the Minister, or no agreement is reached:  
(2) If, after a period of 3 months, — 
 (a) the owner fails to respond to any invitation issued under 
subsection (1); or  
 
16  For our purposes, a public work includes a government work: see s 2 definition of “public work”.  
17  See s 20.

(b) the owner refuses to negotiate with the Minister or the local 
authority, as the case may be; or  
 (c) an agreement for the sale and purchase of the land is not made 
with the owner under  section 17,—  
the Minister or local authority may, within 1 year after notifying the 
owner under subsection (1), proceed to take the land under this Act.  
It will be seen that in any of these circumstances the Minister may proceed to take the 
land, provided that is done within one year after notifying the owner of the Minister’s 
desire to acquire the land.  
[20] We have noted that, having issued a notice of desire to acquire, the Minister 
must provide the owner with a valuation and advise them of the estimated amount of 
compensation to which they would be entitled under the Act.  Compensation is not 
limited to the value of the land,  which is ordinarily taken to be its value on the open 
market as between a willing seller and a willing buyer at the specified date (which in the case of a compulsory acquisition is the date on which the land vests in the 
Crown).
18  Under s 60 the owner is entitled to “full compensation” for the “acquisition, 
taking, injurious affection, or damage”.19  Section 62 provides that compensation may 
exceed the land’s market value where the compensation relates to a matter not directly 
based on the value of the land and there is a right to compensation under any Act.20  
By way of example, compensation may be provided under the Public Works Act for 
disturbance to land, for losses on repayment of a mortgage, for business losses, and 
for the acquisition of a dwelling used by the owner as a residence.21  Grants of interests 
in land may also be made under pt 6 in lieu of compensation.22 
[21] If the Minister decides to take the land, a notice of intention to take must be 
issued under s 23(1).  The notice must describe the land, the purpose for which it is to be used and the reasons why the taking is considered reasonably necessary.  
The landowner must be served and advised of the period within which they may 
exercise their right to lodge an objection to the taking with the Environment Court.
23 
 
18  Section 62(1)(b) and (2)(a).   
19  Section 60(1) —subject to any other statutory provision otherwise.  
20  Section 62(1)(b)(i).  
21  Sections 66– 68 and 72.  See also s 72C.  
22  Section 103.  
23  See also s  23(3).

[22] A written objection having been received, the Environment Court must notify 
the Minister, who must reply with specified information: the authority under which it 
is proposed to take the land; the nature of the work to be constructed or the purpose 
for whic h the land is required; and “such other matters as may be appropriate having 
regard to the objections made and to any practice directions issued by the Environment  Court”.
24  The Court must inquire into the objection and the intended 
taking, and it must conduct a hearing for that purpose.25  Section 24(7) provides that:  
(7) The Environment Court shall — 
 (a) ascertain the objectives of the Minister or local authority, as 
the case may require:  
 (b) enquire into the adequacy of the consideration given to alternative sites, routes, or other methods of achieving those objectives:  
 (c) in its discretion, send the matter back to the Minister or local authority for further consideration in the light of any 
directions given by the court:  
 (d) decide whether, in its opinion, it would be fair, sound, and 
reasonably necessary for achieving the objectives of the 
Minister or local authority, as the case may require, for the 
land of the objector to be taken:  
 (e) prepare a written report on the objection and on the court’s 
findings:  
 (f) submit its report and findings to the Minister or local 
authority, as the case may require.  
[23] In short, the Environment Court must:  
(a) ascertain the Minister’s objectives and inquire into the consideration 
given to alternative sites, routes or other methods of achieving those 
objectives; and  
(b) decide whether in its opinion it would be “fair, sound, and reasonably necessary” to take the land to achieve the Minister’s objectives; and  
 
24  Section 24(1) –(2). 
25  Section 24(3).

(c) prepare and submit to the Minister, and copy to the objector,26 a report 
on the objection and on the Court’s findings. 
The Court’s report and findings bind the Minister.27 
[24] The Environment Court’s jurisdiction does not extend to fixing the amount of 
compensation.28  That is provided for under pt 5 of the Act, which provides that in the 
absence of agreement the owner is entitled to apply to the Minister for a formal offer 
of compensation, and to claim compensation from the Minister, and to file the claim 
in the Distr ict Court for hearing in the Land Valuation Tribunal, which may fix 
compensation.29  Such a claim may be made, relevantly, at any time after the Minister 
has issued a notice of desire to acquire the land under s  18(1)(a).30  Nor is it necessary 
that compensation be finally fixed before the taking is complete. 
[25] A taking is effected by Proclamation under s 26.  That section provides that the 
Minister must be satisfied that the land should be taken for the public work specified 
in the s 23 notice and that “no private injury will be done for which due compensation 
is not provided” in the Act.31  If so, the Minister may recommend the 
Governor -General to issue a Proclamation taking the land.32  The land vests in the 
Crown when the taking is completed under s 26.33 
The statutory compass of negotiations  
[26] Negotiations conducted under s 18 are distinctive in three respects.  First, they 
are held in the shadow of a taking.  The owner cannot veto the acquisition, or demand 
a price that assumes a power of veto,34 and because the notice of desire is lodged 
 
26  Section 24(9).  The Court must also make copies available to the public.  
27  Section 24(10).  
28  Nor is the amount of compensation relevant to whether the taking would be fair, sound and 
reasonably necessary: see Trustees of the T & ANH Damerell Family Trust v Whangarei District 
Council [2015] NZEnvC 182, [2015] ELHNZ 250 at [5] –[6]. 
29  See especially Public Works Act, ss 69 –70, 82, 84 and 95.  
30  Section 80(1)(a).  
31  Section 26(1).  
32  Section 26(1)(b).  A survey plan is also required under s 26(1)(a).  Our understanding is that the Minister has not delegated the s 26 power to recommend a Proclamation be issued.  
33  Section 26( 3).  Title formally vests in the Crown on the 14th day after the Proclamation is 
published in the New Zealand Gazette . 
34  Under s 62(1)(a), no value may be attached to the fact that a taking is compulsory except for the 
limited additional compensation available under ss 72– 76.

against the title, the Minister would effectively become a party to any attempt to sell 
the property to a third party.  In the absence of agreement, the Minister may proceed 
to take the land, subject (if the landowner objects) to the Environment Court’s opinion 
that the taking is fair, sound and reasonably necessary.35 
[27] Second, if the land is taken the owner may insist on “full compensation”, fixed 
if necessary by the Land Valuation Tribunal by reference to the market value of the 
land and extending to all heads of loss which are compensable under the Act.36 
[28] Third, the Act does not allow the Minister to fall back immediately on the right 
to take the land and have compensation fixed subsequently.  As the Minister concedes, 
it creates a discrete duty to attempt negotiations.  Negotiations begin with the 
s 18(1)( c) invitation to sell a defined area of land in return for the Minister’s estimation 
of the compensation to which the landowner is entitled.  The legislation is insistent about what happens next; for at least three months the Minister must make “every 
ende avour to negotiate” with the owner “in good faith” in an attempt to reach 
agreement.
37 
[29] We recognise that the requirements of good faith negotiations are likely to vary, 
perhaps substantially, with the circumstances of any given case.  This case is not an opportunity to examine a set of negotiations against the s  18(1)(d) standard, because 
negotiations foundered at the very outset.  What can be said is that the legislation 
points to substantive matters that negotiations must or may address.  The appeal 
requires that we consider where—as the Minister saw it— responsibility for 
substantive negot iations ultimately lay: was it with LINZ, or NZTA, or with TPG as 
an agent; and if the latter, was TPG’s principal LINZ or NZTA?  
[30] Negotiations may include the requirement for the land and, where the Minister 
does not propose to take all of the owner’s land, the extent and location of any taking.  
That is so because in the absence of agreement, and assuming an objection, the 
Environme nt Court will decide whether the taking is fair, sound and reasonably 
 
35  As noted above, the Court’s report and findings bind the Minister: s 24(10).  On its terms s 26(1) 
is not available if the Court allows the objection.  
36  See above at [20] . 
37  See above at [18] –[19].

necessary to meet the Minister’s objectives.  The Court’s power to assess the 
requirement creates an opportunity and an incentive to negotiate matters such as the 
area required, its location on the owner’s land, and any mitigation measures that may 
allevia te the impact of the taking on the owner’s enjoyment of their remaining land. 
[31] In addition, negotiations obviously include compensation.  The Act fixes the 
measure of the land’s value —market value—but that is a matter of opinion on which 
reasonable valuers may disagree, and the Crown (acting through the Minister) must also compensate the owner for other heads of loss recognised by the Act.  
Delegation of the Minister’s functions and powers  
[32] As we have said, the Minister concedes that the duty to negotiate under 
s 18(1)(d) is discrete, meaning that it is separate from the Minister’s powers to issue a 
notice of desire to acquire under s  18(1)(a) and to issue a notice of intention to take 
under s 23(1).  The duty to comply with s  18(1)(d) is non- delegable,
38 meaning that 
the Minister is always accountable for the exercise of the power or function of conducting negotiations, whether they are conducted by the Minister personally or by 
their delegate.  
[33] The Minister’s powers are generally delegable under s  4C(1) of the Act.  
However,  an exception is made for  the Minister’s s 23(1) power to issue a notice of 
intention to take land; s 4C(2) goes on to prohibit delegation of that power.  
Delegation of powers and functions within the Public Service  
[34] Under s  4C a Minister may delegate “any of the powers” conferred on the 
Minister by the Act to “any officer of the Minister’s department”.
39  On its face this 
provision is confined to delegation of powers, and it does not authorise delegation 
outside the Minister’s department.  
 
38  None of the provisions discussed below at [33] –[35] permit the delegation of the Minister’s duties.  
Compare Resource Management Act, s  29(1).  
39  Public Works Act, s 4C(1).  The delegation must be in writing.  The Minister may not delegate the 
power of delegation conferred by the section.

[35] However, powers of delegation in the State Sector Act 1988 (now repealed and 
replaced, but in force at the relevant time) were also applicable.40  Section 28(1) of 
that Act provided that a Minister might delegate “all or any of the Minister’s functions 
and powers” to the chief executive of the Minister’s department,41 and under s  41(1) 
the chief executive might delegate further, either generally or particularly, with the 
Minister’s prior written approval.  The chief executive’s delegate might then delegate 
further under s  41(2), with the chief executive’s prior written approval.  
Any Public  Service employee might be a delegate under these general powers of 
delegation.42 
[36] The Public Service was (and is) defined as comprising specified departments 
and departmental agencies.43  These include LINZ.  As noted earlier, successive 
ministers and chief executives have exercised the power of delegation under s 41(1).  Because these delegations are not in dispute, we may confine ourselves to examples.
44   
[37] On 14 July 2009, the then Minister for Land Information delegated to the 
Chief  Executive of LINZ certain specified functions and powers.  They included 
s 18(1)(d) of the Public Works Act, which was summarised as “[e]ndeavour to 
negotiate”, subs  (1)(a) (“[s ]erving notice of desire to take land for a public work”) and 
subs (1)(c) (“invite owner to sell”).  The Minister authorised subdelegation of these 
functions to “employees of Land Information New Zealand”.  We observe that these 
delegations treated negotia tion under s  18(1)(d) as a discrete function which is related 
but not subsidiary to other s  18 functions. 
[38] On 29 November 2019, the Chief Executive subdelegated the same powers to 
the Deputy Chief Executive Crown Property and authorised further subdelegation.  
 
40  The State Sector Act 1988 applied until 7 August 2020 and was replaced by the Public Service 
Act.  Relevant  provisions were carried over into the Public Service Act in essentially the same 
terms: see sch 6 cls 2 –6.  Unless stated otherwise, these reasons  refer to the general powers of 
delegation following the 2013 amendments made by the State Sector Amendment Act 2013  and 
State Sector Amendment Act (No 2) 2013 . 
41  Excluding the power of delegation itself, and subject to any limiting provision otherwise in another 
Act: State Sector Act, s  28(3) –(4). 
42  See s  41(1A).  
43  Section 27; and see s 27A( 1) definitions of “department” and “departmental agency”, and sch 1.  
See now Public Service Act, s  10, which also includes in the “public service” interdepartmental 
executive boards  and interdepartmental ventures.  See also below n 49.  
44  On the evidence before us, there have been a number of sets of delegations since 2009.

On the same day, the Deputy Chief Executive subdelegated the same powers and 
functions to the persons holding specified senior positions within LINZ. 
Delegation outside the Public Service  
[39] Section 41(2A) of the State Sector Act also authorised a chief executive to 
subdelegate ministerial functions or powers to a person outside the Public Service, but 
that power of delegation was limited to “a clearly identified function or power” and it 
migh t be exercised only after the chief executive had obtained the appropriate 
Minister’s prior written approval and satisfied themselves that any potential conflicts of interest would be avoided or managed.  The subdelegation was to be noted in the 
department ’s annual report, with a description of the effectiveness with which the 
delegated function or power was performed or exercised.
45 
[40] For purposes of the State Sector Act, a delegation to NZTA employees would 
be a delegation outside the Public Service.46  NZTA’s function, in brief, is that of 
managing the land transport system, including state highways, in the public interest.47  
Managing includes planning, design, funding and construction.48  Land which it 
requires for that purpose qualifies as a Government work under the Public Works Act.  
On acquisition, the land vests in the Crown.  However, NZTA is not part of the 
Public  Service.49  It is a Crown entity established under the Crown Entities Act 2004.50  
[41] A subdelegation to TPG staff would also be a delegation outside the 
Public  Service.  TPG is a limited liability company owned and operated by people who 
are not government employees.51  As NZTA is not part of the Public Service, an NZTA 
 
45  State Sector Act, s  41(2C).  
46  We do not need to consider whether these provisions might also allow for delegation to NZTA or 
TPG themselves as “persons”: see Legislation Act, s  13 definition of “person”.  The same analysis

[46] LINZ has published a Standard for the acquisition of land under the

What is the issue for decision?  
[71] The approved question for which leave was granted was:
77

Public  Works Act 1981, may be undertaken by an accredited contractor rather 
than by the Respondent personally (or an official of Toitū Te Whenua | Land Information New Zealand with delegated authority by the Respondent).  
[72] It is not in dispute that the Minister’s negotiations function may be 
subdelegated by the Chief Executive to an accredited supplier under s  41(2A) of the 
State Sector Act (now sch 6 cl 2(5) of the Public Service Act), as we have explained 
above at [39] .  The requirements of that section would have to be met: the Minister’s 
written approval must first be obtained, the function or power must be clearly identified and any conflicts of interest dealt with, and reporting requirements must be 
met. 
[73] The Chief Executive might also subdelegate the negotiations function to a 
delegate within NZTA, but the same formal requirements would have to be met.  
And as we have noted above, the NZTA delegate could not then further subdelegate 
the function to anyone else, including a person within TPG.

The Court of Appeal  
[80] The Court of Appeal rejected the Pascoes’ submission that the elements of the 
Minister’s negotiations function must be exercised by an authorised delegate.85  
Rather,  it was necessary to ask whether the element or task was of a kind that could 
only be carried out by a person with statutory authority to do so, having regard to 
public law principles and the specific statutory context.  Put another way, it was 
necessary to ask whether engaging someone to perform the particular task amounted 
to delegation of the statutory function.86 
[81] The Court reasoned that negotiation is an administrative or supporting function 
that need not be formally delegated and may be contracted out to a third party, subject 
to the negotiator being appropriately qualified, selected, instructed, supervised and 
controlled, and the requirement that the negotiator report back on material decisions 
they have made during negotiations which may have shaped the ultimate outcome.87 
[82] The Court accepted that good faith negotiations require more than just 
information -gathering or consultation and saw force in Ms Gepp’s submission that 
negotiations are a fluid and path- dependent process, but reasoned that this did not 
require that negotia tions must be conducted by the Minister or delegate .  Rather, it 
may be that some matters are so fundamental to the shape of the negotiation that they 
should be referred back to the decision- maker along the way or at least expressly 
identified in any final  report as material decisions made during negotiations that may 
have shaped the ultimate outcome.88 
[83] Indeed, the Court held, to require a LINZ official to conduct day -to-day 
negotiations would not make good practical sense because it is the acquiring agency

which is responsible for the project, understands where there may be room for 
flexibility in terms of the scope and timing of land acquired, and can assess the impact 
of these alternatives on the project.89  The Court pointed out that it is commonplace 
for a principal to have an authorised person, such as a solicitor experienced in conveyancing, negotiate an agreement for the principal’s approval.  
[84] The Court concluded that the Minister may make every endeavour to negotiate 
in good faith by appointing as negotiator an appropriately skilled and knowledgeable 
person, so long as the Minister retains ultimate responsibility for the negotiation.
90  
It is necessary that the Minister do so because a landowner’s rights and interests will 
be affected by the fact that negotiations were attempted but were unsuccessful, 
potentially triggering significant statutory powers which only the Minister or a 
deleg ate may exercise.   
Was the negotiations function delegated outside the Public Service?  
[85] We turn to whether LINZ officials informally delegated the negotiations 
function outside the Public Service.  By informal, we mean that the function was 
delegated without following the necessary statutory processes.  
[86] We frame the question in that way because, if the function was delegated, the 
corporate identity of the delegate (let alone that of the individual) is not clear from the 
record before us.  It may have been NZTA or TPG.  TPG was appointed by NZTA and 
may ha ve acted as its agent.  For our purposes it is sufficient to decide whether the 
Minister’s LINZ delegates subdelegated the Minister’s negotiations function to 
someone outside the Public Service.  If so, the subdelegation was unauthorised.91 
Refusal to negotiate? 
[87] We observe that  if the Pascoes refused to negotiate for purposes of s 18(2), as 
the Minister pleads, it must have been in the sense that to refuse to negotiate with TPG, 
as LINZ officials told them to do in the notice of desire served on 31 August 2020,

was to refuse to negotiate with the Minister.  That contention rests on the proposition 
that the Minister’s LINZ delegates actually performed the negotiations function and 
engaged TPG to assist them.  
The role played by LINZ officials  
[88] We have explained that LINZ officials saw it as their responsibility to issue the 
notice of desire to acquire, and TPG reported to them in connection with that 
decision.92  The s 18 report nominated an estimate of the compensation to which the 
Pascoes were entitled.  Relying on that report, LINZ officials issued the notice of desire to acquire and the invitation to negotiate.  The notice contained what amounted 
to the Minis ter’s starting offer, expressed as an estimate of the compensation to which 
the Pascoes were entitled.  
[89] Had the negotiations succeeded, TPG would have presented LINZ officials 
with an offer to sell signed by the Pascoes and approved by NZTA.  It would have been accompanied by a report under s 17.  As explained above, under LINZ processes 
that report  would explain why the land was required, summarise negotiations, and 
offer an opinion about how the proposed payment related to the owner’s entitlements.
93 
[90] These processes establish that at the beginning and end of a successful 
negotiation process, LINZ would approve the requirement and the terms, including 
compensation.  LINZ would also approve the terms on which the Minister proposed 
to take the land if negotiations failed and it was necessary to invoke s 23.94   
[91] We accept that to this extent LINZ delegates remained responsible both for 
ensuring that negotiations happened and for the outcome.  Under LINZ processes it is 
theoretically possible, for example, that officials might reject an offer presented to 
them unde r s 17 on the ground that the accompanying report showed that the owners 
had agreed to accept less than their entitlement, or that the acquiring agency had 
offered terms which were unduly generous.

[92] But to recognise that possibility is not to conclude that LINZ officials 
performed the negotiations function.  We have explained that s 18 treats negotiation 
as a function distinct from both the notice of desire to acquire and the execution of an 
agreement for sale and purchase under s  17.95  The notice of desire commences the 
minimum period of three months to attempt negotiations.  The presentation of an offer for the Minister’s approval follows their successful completion.  The negotiations 
function is performed between those two points in time.  Consistent with that, LINZ 
processes envisage that the documentation will take the form of an owner’s offer to 
sell to the Minister on terms that have been agreed with the Crown acquiring agency.
96  
LINZ performs a reviewing or compliance role before executing the agreement.  
[93] We do not agree with the Court of Appeal that negotiating is a subsidiary or 
supporting function.97  The Act expresses an unmistakeable preference that land be 
acquired for public works by agreement with owners.  It is sensitive to loss of owners’ 
autonomy vis -à-vis their land.  Compulsory acquisition is possible only when 
negotiations have failed.98  The Act also envisages that negotiation may crystallise 
both the requirement and the owners’ entitlement to compensation.99 
The Minister’ s delegate may employ an agent, so long as the delegate remains party 
to the negotiation 
[94] We agree with the Court of Appeal that nothing precludes the Minister or 
delegate from engaging a person who is not themselves a delegate to assist in 
performance of the negotiations function.100  To do so is not to further delegate the 
function itself.  The  person who performs a supporting task assigned to them by the 
delegate is not themselves exercising a statutory power or function.  That being so, it 
was not necessary to follow the delegation processes set out in the State Sector Act 
before engaging an accredited supplier such as TPG to assist the Minister’s delegates.  
As the Court of Appeal observed, nothing in the scheme of the legislation precludes

[95] We also agree with the Court of Appeal that the obligation to use every 
endeavour to negotiate in good faith with the owner goes to how the negotiations are 
conducted, rather than who conducts them.103  The obligation to seek agreement will 
require that attempts be made to identify and address the landowner’s concerns and 
needs and that genuine consideration be given to the landowner’s proposals.  As we 
recognised above at [29], it is not possible to say in the abstract what level of 
involvement may be required of the Minister or delegate.  All that can be said is that 
the legislation envisages the delegate must ensure that every endeavour is made to 
negotiate in good faith within parameters set by the statutory scheme for taking and 
compensation.  It  may be, as the Court of Appeal also observed, that some proposals 
may be so unrealistic, having regard to the Act’s compensation provisions, that they 
do not require in- depth consideration.104 
What happened in this case amounted to delegation of the negotiations function 
[96] We accept the Pascoes’ and Ms  Gepp’s argument that what happened here 
amounted to complete delegation by LINZ of the negotiations function to a person or 
persons outside the Public Service.  We see that conclusion as almost inevitable once 
it is accepted t hat negotiation is a discrete function, not merely subsidiary to the 
execution of agreements for sale and purchase or administrative, and that the role to be played by LINZ officials was confined here to reviewing the work done by others 
for compliance wit h the statutory scheme and LINZ’s own processes.  
[97] To elaborate on the latter point, the record suggests that LINZ officials would 
play no role in the negotiations that would precede an offer to sell or a notice of 
intention to take, had the Pascoes done as LINZ directed and negotiated with TPG.

matters that would be the subject of negotiations, that LINZ officials had not 
supervised that process, and that they meant to play no part  in it.  
[98] It follows that the Pascoes did not refuse to negotiate with the Minister by 
refusing to negotiate with TPG following receipt of the Minister’s notice of desire on 
31 August 2020.  TPG was representing NZTA with respect to negotiations, and 
neither NZTA nor TPG held the necessary delegated authority.  
Relief  
[99] We will remit the proceeding to the High Court, which will need to reconsider, 
in light of  our judgment, its decision to dismiss the first and second causes of action 
in light of any further findings of fact that that Court thinks necessary to determine the appropriate relief.
109 
[100]  The relief sought by the Pascoes is an order revoking the s  23 notice, a 
declaration that the Minister or his lawful delegate must attempt negotiations, and a 
declaration that NZTA and TPG are not lawful delegates under s  4C(1) of the 
Public  Works Act.

[101]  We record that it need not follow from our findings that the s 23 notice will be 
set aside.  That is a matter for the High Court to decide in light of further information.  
Relief in judicial review is discretionary.  Events appear to have moved on in ways  
that may influence the exercise of the discretion.  For example, there may now be no prospect of the route changing. 
[102]  We also observe that the question of compensation is still at large.  As we 
explained above, it will be fixed as at the date of taking, which has yet to happen, and 
if called upon the Land Valuation Tribunal will do the fixing by reference to statutory 
entitlements.
110 
[103]  In the meantime, it is customary in judicial review to allow the decision -maker 
an opportunity to remedy an error.  Remittal to the High Court will give the Minister 
an opportunity to invite the Pascoes to negotiate with a duly authorised delegate.  
(For t he avoidance of doubt, that may be someone from NZTA or TPG, provided 
formal delegation processes are followed.)  The  High  Court may take any attempted 
negotiations into account when deciding whether relief is warranted.  
Disposition  
[104]  The Court answers the question for which leave was granted in the following 
way: the negotiations function under s  18(1)(d) of the Public Works Act may be 
performed by an accredited contractor, rather than the Minister personally or the Minister’s delegate  within LINZ, provided the accredited contractor holds delegated 
authority to perform that function. 
[105]  The Court finds that LINZ officials delegated the negotiations function outside 
the Public Service in this case but did not follow the necessary statutory processes.  
It is not possible, on the information before us, to say to whom the function was 
delegat ed.  It  is common ground that no one in NZTA or TPG held delegated authority, 
and that LINZ officials insisted the Pascoes deal with TPG.  That being so, the Pascoes 
did not refuse to negotiate, for purposes of s  18(2), by refusing to deal with TPG.

ELLEN FRANCE AND KÓS JJ  
[108]  This appeal arises from preliminary questions identified by the High Court in 
the course of addressing a judicial review brought by the Pascoes challenging a notice 
given by the Minister for Land Information under s  23 of the Public Works Act 1981 
(PWA).  The High Court’s answer to the relevant preliminary question was:112 
Is s 18(1)(d) of the [PWA] complied with if the relevant negotiations with the 
land owner are undertaken by an entity accredited by [ Toitū Te Whenua | Land 
Information New Zealand (LINZ) ] as a Crown Property Accredited Supplier 
(such as [The Property Group Ltd ( TPG )]) rather than the Minister or his 
delegate, provided it is the Minister or his delegate who exercises the ultimate 
power to enter into an agreement to acquire or proceed to take the land?  
The Minister is not required to personally carry out the operational tasks, 
including the negotiations referred to under s  18(1)(d) of the [ PWA ], but is 
entitled to satisfy himself that those have been undertaken by staff or 
contractors in compliance with s  18(1)(d) for the purposes of exercising the 
power under the Act to enter into an agreement to acquire or proceed to take 
land.  
[109]  The Court of Appeal agreed with that conclusion, holding that negotiations for 
the purposes of s  18(1)(d) of the PWA may  be conducted “by the Minister, a delegate 
of the Minister, or an authorised representative of the Crown such as TPG ”.113  
That negotiator need not hold a formal delegation , but “ there must be systems in place 
to ensure sufficient oversight by the Minister (or delegate) of the negotiations, and to 
ensure the Minister (or delegate) receives sufficient information about the negotiation 
process to support that conclusion” .114

[110]  Leave was granted by this Court on a rephrased question:115 
… whether the Court of Appeal was correct to find that negotiations prior to 
the compulsory acquisition of land for essential works, under s  18 of the 
[PWA] , may be undertaken by an accredited contractor rather than by the 
[Minister] personally (or an official of [LINZ] with delegated authority by the 
[Minister]).  
[111]  For the reasons given by the majority, the answer to this question, on any view, 
is “Yes”.116  That would be sufficient to determine the appeal. 
[112]  In their reasons, the majority reach three key conclusions:  
(a) The Minister could lawfully have delegated the negotiations  function  
to the New  Zealand Transport Agency | Waka Kotahi  (NZTA) or TPG, 
but did not do so.117 
(b) The negotiations  function is discrete, not a subsidiary or supporting 
function, but the delegate may engage an agent, as long as the delegate 
remains party to the negotiation.118 
(c) In breach of point  (b), here there was an unauthorised de facto 
delegation, given the formal delegate ( LINZ  officials)  did not remain 
party to the negotiation.119 
[113]  We agree with point (a) for the reasons given by the majority.  We depart from 
the majority at point (b) and at point (c).  
[114]  We address point (b) first.  On this aspect, we agree with the Court of Appeal 
that just because a person is engaged to assist with the performance of a function, it does not necessarily follow there has been a delegation to that person.

the extent of decision- making authority and control retained by the office holder” as 
well as “the nature of the task that the third party is engaged to perform”.121 
[115]  Our view on point (b) accords with that of the Court of Appeal:  
[119]  Neither the Pascoes nor Ms Gepp identified any public law principle 
of general application that would require the Minister to negotiate personally 
or through an authorised representative.  We consider that negotiations 
towards a contract with a landowner are properly seen as administrative or 
supporting functions of a kind that can be performed by a person other than 
the relevant statutory decision -maker, provided the decision -maker retains 
control over the manner in which the negotiations are conduct ed, and retains 
responsibility for deciding whether to enter into a contract, and, if so, on what 
terms.  Retaining TPG (or another accredited supplier) to conduct s  18(1)(d) 
negotiations does not in our view involve delegation of a function or power in the relevant sense.  
[116]  We therefore consider the negotiations function may properly be performed by 
a person other than the relevant statutory decision- maker, as an administrative or 
supporting function, provided the decision- maker retains control over the conduct of 
the negotia tions and responsibility for deciding whether to enter into a contract (and 
on what terms).  Our difficulty with then determining the nature of the engagement 
here is apparent from our response to point (c), as we now explain.  We make three 
points about point  (c). 
[117]  First, in our view the better analysis of the evidence is that no valid negotiation 
has yet occurred.  Neither party approached the bargaining process with the correct 
legal framework in focus.  The s 18(1)(a) notice of desire to acquire land was lawful.  
The Pascoes’ response to it was to ignore TPG and to insist they would not negotiate 
with anyone other than the Minister or someone formally delegated to negotiate with 
them by the Minister —and not TPG.  That demand (and refusal) was unsound as a 
matter of  law.  LINZ officials, who in fact held the necessary delegation from the 
Minister, clearly intended to hand all or most of the negotiation responsibility over to 
TPG.122  That may have exceeded the limits we acknowledge above at [115] –[116]  
and would then have been an abdication of their delegated statutory function.

[118]  Secondly, it cannot be said with sufficient certainty at this point that had the

The uncertainty is encapsulated in this passage from the majority’s reasons:123 
[97] To elaborate on the latter point, the record suggests  that LINZ officials

[119]  Thirdly, given both parties were operating under fundamental legal 
misconceptions, that the negotiation process therefore misfired altogether, that lawful 
negotiations may yet be completed, and that we that we are dealing with preliminary

to answer the approved question in the affirmative. 
[120]  We would dismiss the appeal, but make no order for costs.
